title: On the Emergence of Pendular Structure in Multi-Contact Locomotion
abstract: LIPM（线性倒立摆模型）在腿式运动控制中无处不在，但几乎总是作为建模选择而非控制器成本函数实际偏好的结果。本文试图更明确地建立这一关联。通过研究一个惩罚角动量变化率的小型质心最优控制问题，我们观察其最优解的典型形态。主要发现有三点：在全支撑相中，最优解会以由力矩雅可比矩阵奇异值分解（SVD）确定的速率向摆动力模式漂移，该常数由足距几何决定，与实验结果的误差在16%以内；对于像对角小跑这样的双足支撑相，摩擦锥为角动量变化率引入了一个下界，这是任何权重调节都无法消除的；同时，在某一临界水平加速度处会出现非光滑的可行性拐点，该临界值可解析表达。当添加要求非零角动量变化率的任务项时，最优解会以可预测的方式偏离摆线模式。这些发现与经典的ZMP（零力矩点）/DCM（动态捕获点）理论并无实质性差异。我们在点质量四足机器人和MuJoCo环境中的宇树Go1机器人上验证了这些结论（分别采用开环QP和力矩级闭环控制器），并指出了渐近性分析在何种情况下不再能准确描述闭环系统的实际行为。

Abstract
LIPM is everywhere in legged-locomotion control, but almost always as a
modeling choice rather than as something the controller’s cost actually
prefers.
This note tries to make that link more explicit.
Working from a small centroidal OCP that penalizes the rate of angular
momentum, we look at what its optimum tends to look like.
Three things come out.
With full-rank stance, the optimum drifts toward a pendular force pattern at
a rate determined by the SVD of the moment Jacobian; the constant is set by
foot-span geometry and matches the experiments to within
∼
16

the pendular set in a predictable way.
None of this is far from the classical ZMP/DCM picture.
We test these claims on a point-mass quadruped and on the Unitree Go1 in
MuJoCo (open-loop QP and a torque-level closed-loop controller), and we
note where the asymptotic story stops being a good description of what the
closed loop actually does.

VII

A few comments on what the analysis does and does not say.
The story is cleanest when the moment Jacobian has full row rank.
There the optimum sits near the pendular set, the rate is set by the SVD of
that Jacobian, and ZMP
[

This is also where multi-contact OCPs that don’t ask for any particular
angular-momentum behavior keep producing pendular-looking solutions—the
cost structure, not the model class, is doing the work.
For trot-style stances the picture is different.
The rank-

constraint on top—the kink of Theorem

preview_text:
LIPM is everywhere in legged-locomotion control, but almost always as a modeling choice rather than as something the
  controller's cost actually prefers. This note tries to make that link more explicit. Working from a small centroidal
  OCP that penalizes the rate of angular momentum, we look at what its optimum tends to look like. Three things come
  out. With full-rank stance, the optimum drifts toward a pendular force pattern at a rate determined by the SVD of the
  moment Jacobian; the constant is set by foot-span geometry and matches the experiments to within 16%. With N=2 stance,
  as in trot, the friction cone introduces a lower bound on $\|\dot{H}_G\|$ that no amount of weight tuning fixes; we
  also see a non-smooth feasibility kink at a critical horizontal acceleration that we can write in closed form. Adding
  a task term that asks for a nonzero $\dot{H}_G$ moves the optimum off the pendular set in a predictable way. None of
  this is far from the classical ZMP/DCM picture. We test these claims on a point-mass quadruped and on the Unitree Go1
  in MuJoCo (open-loop QP and a torque-level closed-loop controller), and we note where the asymptotic story stops being
  a good description of what the closed loop actually does.

On the Emergence of Pendular Structure
in Multi-Contact Locomotion
